af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. HaveIBeenPwned API for checking email/password breach status. API key required for account breach lookups; Pwned Passwords endpoint uses k-Anonymity model (no key needed). Rate limits: 1500ms between requests for free tier, up to 5/sec on paid. k-Anonymity for password checks — only first 5 chars of SHA1 hash sent, preserving privacy. Good for security-aware agent workflows. Breach data is read-only and immutable. Simple to integrate.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key for account breach lookups. k-Anonymity model for password checks — privacy-by-design. Troy Hunt maintained. GDPR compliant for breach data handling. API key via environment variable. Breach data contains PII (email addresses) — appropriate data governance required. k-Anonymity ensures passwords are never sent in plaintext. Read-only breach intelligence platform.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. Stable API versioning. Good backward compatibility. Retry guidance documented. Rate limiting (1500ms between free tier requests) requires careful pacing for bulk email checks. Full idempotency on breach queries — same email always returns same results for a given dataset. New breach additions make responses non-deterministic over time. Throttling required for bulk checks.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Healthcare practice management platform. GraphQL API — agents must learn GraphQL querying. API key or OAuth2 depending on integration type. HIPAA compliance required for PHI access. Patient, appointment, form, and billing management. Rate limits documented but not prominently. EHR data requires careful handling per HIPAA. Telehealth and scheduling are primary agent use cases. Limited public documentation — detailed docs behind developer portal login.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key or OAuth2 for authentication. HIPAA BAA available for PHI access — required for production healthcare workflows. Healthie maintained. SOC2/HIPAA compliance for healthcare platform. API key via environment variable. PHI access requires strict access controls and audit logging. GraphQL mutations can modify patient data — agents need safeguards.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with SLA for healthcare platform. Stable GraphQL API schema. Migration guides available. Retry guidance documented. GraphQL schema changes require query updates. Appointment operations have real-world consequences — careful idempotency handling required. Rate limits constrain bulk patient data operations. Healthcare workflows require reliable error handling to prevent patient care disruption.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. LLM observability and logging platform — tracks API calls to OpenAI, Anthropic, and other providers. Simple API key. Acts as a proxy between agent and LLM provider. Request/response logging with latency, cost, and token tracking. Custom properties for tagging requests. Rate limiting and caching features. Feedback collection for fine-tuning. Excellent for monitoring LLM agent workflows. Zero-config integration via proxy endpoint.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Simple API key — no scope granularity. Helicone maintained. No formal compliance certifications listed. API key via environment variable. All LLM requests pass through Helicone proxy — prompts and responses are logged. Review data retention policy for sensitive agent prompts. Self-hosted option available for data privacy requirements. Helicone sees all LLM traffic — significant trust consideration.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. Stable API versioning. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Proxy architecture means Helicone downtime blocks all LLM calls — consider fallback routing. Cache hits improve agent response latency. Request logging is eventual consistent — real-time dashboards may lag. Async logging mode reduces latency impact on LLM calls.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. HelloSign (now Dropbox Sign) e-signature API. API key (simple for agents) or OAuth2. Send signature requests programmatically. Signed documents generate webhooks — must expose HTTPS endpoint for production. Rate limits documented. Signature requests are irreversible once sent — agents need confirmation flows. Test mode available (signatures not legally binding). Brand as Dropbox Sign — migration from HelloSign branding in progress. Similar to DocuSign but simpler auth.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key or OAuth2. Dropbox maintained. SOC2/GDPR/ESIGN/UETA compliance for e-signature platform. API key via environment variable. Webhook HMAC signature verification required for production security. Signature requests are legally binding — agents need safeguards before sending. Test mode for safe agent development. Limited scope granularity on API keys.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with SLA. Stable API versioning (HelloSign to Dropbox Sign rebranding managed carefully). Migration guides available. Retry guidance documented. Signature request creation not idempotent — retrying creates duplicate requests. Webhook delivery reliable with retry. Completed signature callbacks require HTTPS endpoint. Rebranding from HelloSign to Dropbox Sign required client ID migration.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Help Scout customer support platform. OAuth2 only — no API key option makes agent auth complex. Conversation management, customer profiles, mailboxes. Rate limits documented (200/min per OAuth app). Webhook support for conversation events. Conversation creation not idempotent — retrying creates duplicates. Mailbox IDs required for all operations. Good for automated customer support agent workflows but OAuth2 setup required.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. OAuth2 only — no API key option. Limited scope granularity on OAuth scopes. Help Scout maintained. GDPR compliance for customer data. OAuth tokens via secure flow. Customer support data contains PII — appropriate data governance required. No read-only access token option — all tokens have write access. OAuth token refresh must be implemented for long-running agents.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with SLA. Stable API versioning. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Rate limit 200/min per OAuth app — design agents accordingly. Conversation creation not idempotent — check for existing conversations before creating. OAuth token refresh required for long-running agents. Mailbox ID discovery required at setup. Webhook HMAC verification recommended.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. HERE Location Services API — geocoding, routing, places, traffic, weather. API key or OAuth2 (OAuth2 for premium services). Route calculation, isoline (drive-time) analysis. Real-time traffic data available. Freemium model with generous free tier. Good alternative to Google Maps. HERE Maps JavaScript API is client-side only — use REST APIs for server-side agent workflows. Place search with category filtering. Multi-modal routing (drive, walk, transit).

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key or OAuth2 for premium services. Limited scope granularity. HERE (Nokia subsidiary) maintained. GDPR compliance. API key via environment variable. API key restriction by app ID and code available. Location query data may raise privacy considerations — review data retention policy. No formal compliance certifications listed beyond GDPR.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with SLA and global infrastructure. Stable versioned REST APIs. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Full idempotency on geocoding and routing queries. Traffic data has freshness bounds — real-time queries always fetched fresh. Freemium rate limits apply — monitor for production scale. Multi-modal routing availability varies by region.

af_score_components.notes: Local MCP server (quality 72) — no auth required. Operates locally without network exposure. No rate limits (local tool). Documentation is moderate — covers core functionality. Error handling for edge cases is limited. Good for local automation workflows that don't require cloud connectivity. Specific capabilities depend on implementation (messaging, transport, or utility functions). Suitable for development and local agent workflows.

security_score_components.security_notes: Local operation — no network exposure by default. No credentials required or stored. Local file system permissions provide implicit access control. Unknown version and dependency audit status. Read/write operations local only. No compliance certifications. Appropriate for development workflows where local-only operation is acceptable.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted local tool with no SLA. Moderate version stability. No documented breaking change policy. Operations idempotency depends on specific tool functionality. Local process availability required for operation. Good for development use; production stability requires careful testing.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Hetzner Cloud API — excellent cost-effective European cloud infrastructure. Simple API key. Read-only API keys available for monitoring agents. Server provisioning, networking, volumes, load balancers, firewalls. Rate limits: 3600 requests/hour. Async operations require polling action status. Server creation not idempotent — check existence before creating. Excellent documentation with OpenAPI spec. Hetzner is a strong alternative to major cloud providers for cost-conscious agents.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key with read-only mode available for safe monitoring agents. Limited scope granularity beyond read/write split. Hetzner maintained. ISO27001/GDPR compliance (EU data center). API key via environment variable. Cloud infrastructure management has high blast radius — agents can destroy servers. Read-only tokens recommended for observability-only workflows. GDPR-compliant European data processing.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Cloud SaaS with SLA and status page. Stable versioned API with OpenAPI spec. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Async server operations require polling action status. Rate limit 3600/hour — design batch operations accordingly. Server creation not idempotent — verify non-existence before creating. Floating IP operations idempotent. Excellent reliability for a cost-effective cloud provider.

af_score_components.notes: Early-stage MCP server (quality 42) with minimal auth requirements. Very limited documentation and public information. Rate limits unknown. Low existing security score (30) suggests minimal security hardening. Niche or experimental tool not yet production-ready. Error handling documentation absent. Not recommended for production agent workflows without significant additional evaluation. Monitor for future maturity before adopting.

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS depends on deployment configuration. No or minimal auth required. No scope granularity. Unknown dependency hygiene for early-stage tool. No compliance certifications. No credentials required reduces credential risk but lack of auth is itself a security concern for network-accessible tools. Significant security hardening required before production use.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted with no SLA. Unknown version — early-stage development. No documented breaking change policy. Unknown idempotency behavior. Limited error recovery guidance. High operational risk for production use. Early adopter risk is very significant.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. HeyGen AI video generation API — creates talking avatar videos from text/audio. Simple API key. Video generation is async — agents must poll status or use webhooks. Videos have TTL — must download before expiry. Avatar and voice IDs required before generation — must discover or store. Rate limits by plan tier. Video generation minutes are the primary cost metric. Custom avatars require training time. Deepfake risk — review terms of service for authorized avatar usage.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Simple API key — no scope granularity. HeyGen maintained. No formal compliance certifications listed. API key via environment variable. AI video generation with real people's likenesses carries consent and deepfake concerns — strict ToS compliance required. Generated videos stored on HeyGen servers temporarily — download before TTL expires. Avatar creation requires authorization to use specific likenesses.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. Moderate API versioning. Migration guides available. Retry guidance documented. Async video generation requires polling — implement with appropriate timeouts. Video URLs expire — download immediately after generation. Generation minutes are consumed even on failure in some cases. Queue times vary with platform load. Avatar and voice ID stability varies as HeyGen updates their model library.
